summary: Gruber reacts to the Disney+ announcement: $7/month, no ads, massive back catalog including Marvel, Pixar, Star Wars, and newly acquired Fox properties like The Simpsons. He argues the price point is a remarkable deal compared to Netflix's $13/month and especially attractive for families with young kids. He's confident Disney+ will be available on Apple devices, noting Disney CEO Bob Iger sits on Apple's board. He also speculates that Apple TV+ will likely be bundled with other Apple subscriptions rather than competing head-on at a premium price point.
key_points: At $7/month with no ads, Disney+ is positioned as a significantly better value than Netflix's $13/month, especially given its deep catalog from Disney, Marvel, Pixar, Star Wars, and Fox
Disney+ and Netflix aren't either/or — most subscribers will pay for both, but cost-conscious consumers will favor Disney's offering
Disney+ will almost certainly be on Apple devices; the only question is whether sign-ups happen on-device or require going through the web
Apple TV+ is not central to Apple the way Disney+ is central to Disney, suggesting Apple will bundle its streaming service rather than compete on standalone pricing
money_quotes: $7 is about what it costs for a small box of popcorn and a bottle of water at Disney World.
I don't think it's either/or — I'll certainly cough up $70/year in addition to whatever it is I'm paying for Netflix, and I'm sure tens of millions of others will too.
For the cost-conscious or simply those with a low threshold of subscription fatigue, $7/month for Disney+ looks like a much better deal than $13/month for Netflix.
I'll eat my hat if Disney+ isn't available for Apple TV and iOS devices.
Disney+ is central to Disney's future. Apple TV+ is not central to Apple.
I continue to think Apple TV+ will be something they add on for 'free' when you pay for some sort of bundle with other Apple subscriptions.
key_insight: Disney+ at $7/month represents an extraordinary value proposition that doesn't so much threaten Netflix as it reveals how Apple TV+ will need to be bundled rather than sold as a standalone competitor.
